Wastewater Surveillance as a Multivariate Change-Point Problem

library(tidyverse) # for data handling
library(ggplot2) # for plotting
library(knitr) # for rendering vignette + tables
library(canadianmaps) # for mapping canadian cities
library(sf) # for spatial vector data (mapping)
library(maps) # for database of world cities
library(naniar) # for missing data visualization

#multichangepoints from Github
devtools::load_all("~/Dropbox/CANSSI/multi-change-points/")
library(multichangepoints)
set.seed(20260305)

Introduction

Wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) monitors the concentration of pathogen genetic material — such as SARS-CoV-2 RNA — in municipal wastewater. Because viral shedding into sewage can precede clinical case reporting by days to weeks Peng et al (2025), surveillance networks that continuously sample and sequence wastewater at multiple sites can provide early-warning signals of emerging or resurging outbreaks, and identify which sites are effected in order to triage resources.

From a statistical perspective, a national WBE network yields a multivariate data stream: at each time point \(t\) (here, each epidemiological week), we observe a measurement \(X_{tk}\) at each of \(K\) monitoring sites. The surveillance task has two components:

  1. Change-point detection — raise an alarm as soon as the data depart from a baseline regime, controlling a type-I error criterion such as the probability of false alarm (PFA) or the average run length under the null (ARL).

  2. Stream identification — after (or concurrent with) an alarm, determine which sites \(k\) are driving the change, so that public-health resources can be directed appropriately.

The multichangepoints package is designed for exactly this setting. A univariate test supermartingale (TSM) is built for each site, marginal increment sequences are combined across sites (via ProductCombiner, AverageCombiner, or UniversalPortfolioCombiner), and a single ShiryaevRobertsDetector monitors the combined evidence sequentially — stopping as soon as the evidence crosses a threshold that guarantees the desired error control.

Data

The dataset wastewater_aggregate.csv is published by the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC), aggregating weekly wastewater measurements from monitoring sites across Canada.
Each row contains one observation indexed by site by week by pathogen. Key columns:

Column Description
site Monitoring location
province Canadian province / territory
weekstart Monday of the epidemiological week
measureid Pathogen marker (covN2, fluA, fluB, rsv)
covN2 Weekly average viral signal (copies / mL, log10-transformed at source) 
#NB: data dictionary says empty sites indicate aggregates, but there are no empty sites
ww <- read_csv(
  here::here("Data", "wastewater_aggregate.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(weekstart = as.Date(weekstart))
head(ww)
#> # A tibble: 6 × 14
#>   Location site     city   province country EpiYear EpiWeek weekstart  measureid
#>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  <chr>    <chr>     <dbl>   <dbl> <date>     <chr>    
#> 1 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      44 2022-10-30 covN2    
#> 2 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      45 2022-11-06 covN2    
#> 3 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      46 2022-11-13 covN2    
#> 4 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      47 2022-11-20 covN2    
#> 5 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      48 2022-11-27 covN2    
#> 6 Alberton Alberton Alber… Prince … Canada     2022      49 2022-12-04 covN2    
#> # ℹ 5 more variables: w_avg <dbl>, min <dbl>, max <dbl>,
#> #   populationcoverage <dbl>, pruid <dbl>

The data span October 2020 to March 2026, cover 76 monitoring sites across 12 provinces and territories, and include four pathogen markers: covN2, fluA, fluB, rsv.

There are length(unique(ww$site)) sites in total, with varying numbers of observations depending on when monitoring started and whether they are missing any data.

COVID-19 (covN2)

We filter to the SARS-CoV-2 N2 gene target and remove the national-aggregate rows (blank site name), retaining only site-level observations.

cov <- ww %>%
  select(Location, site, province, country, weekstart, measureid, w_avg) %>%
  filter(measureid %in% c("covN2", "rsv"), site != "") %>%
  mutate(province = if_else(province == "", NA_character_, province)) %>%
  pivot_wider(values_from = "w_avg", names_from = "measureid") %>%
  mutate(covid_rsv_ratio = covN2/rsv)

cat(
  "Sites:", n_distinct(cov$site), "\n",
  "Provinces:", n_distinct(cov$province, na.rm = TRUE), "\n",
  "Weeks:", n_distinct(cov$weekstart), "\n",
  "Rows:", nrow(cov), "\n"
)
#> Sites: 75 
#>  Provinces: 11 
#>  Weeks: 280 
#>  Rows: 11272

Sampling locations and number of observations

Sites vary in their number of observations primarily because monitoring started at different times at different sites, but also because some weeks are missing measurements.


# ---- observation counts per site -------------------------------------------
site_counts <- ww %>%
  filter(site != "", measureid %in% c("covN2")) %>%
  group_by(site, city, province) %>%
  summarize(count = n()) %>%
  mutate(city_clean = trimws(city)) 

# ---- geocode: auto-match city names from maps::world.cities ----------------
ca_cities <- world.cities[world.cities$country.etc == "Canada", ]

# joins cities by name and take largest if there are any repeats
site_geo <- site_counts %>%
  left_join(
    ca_cities %>% select(name, lat, long, pop),
    by = c("city_clean" = "name"),
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  ) %>%
  group_by(site) %>%
  slice_max(pop, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

# ---- manual coordinates for the 18 unmatched sites -------------------------
manual_coords <- tribble(
  ~site,                                        ~lat,    ~long,
  "Battery Point",                              46.14,  -60.19,
  "Battleford",                                 52.73, -108.30,
  "Birch Hills",                                52.98, -105.44,
  "City of Charlottetown and Town of Stratford",46.24,  -63.13,
  "Dominion-Bridgeport",                        46.13,  -60.00,
  "Miramichi",                                  47.02,  -65.50,
  "Pasqua",                                     50.58, -103.98,
  "Peel G.E. Booth",                            43.73,  -79.72,
  "Southey",                                    50.95, -104.49,
  "St. John's",                                 47.56,  -52.71,
  "St. Stephen",                                45.20,  -67.28,
  "Trenton",                                    45.61,  -62.63,
  "Vancouver Annacis Island",                   49.17, -122.92,
  "Vancouver Iona Island",                      49.22, -123.21,
  "Vancouver Lions Gate",                       49.35, -123.13,
  "Vancouver Lulu Island",                      49.14, -123.19,
  "Vancouver Northwest Langley",                49.17, -122.60,
  "\u00cele-\u00e0-la-Crosse",                  55.45, -107.89
)

site_geo <- site_geo %>%
  rows_update(manual_coords, by = "site", unmatched = "ignore") %>%
  filter(!is.na(lat))

# ---- project points into the canadianmaps Lambert CRS ----------------------
lcc_crs <- "+proj=lcc +lat_1=49 +lat_2=77 +lon_0=-91.52 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +datum=NAD83 +units=m +no_defs"

sites_sf <- site_geo %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("long", "lat"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_transform(crs = lcc_crs)

# ---- map -------------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot() +
  geom_prov(fill = "gray93", colour = "gray60", size = 0.25) +
  geom_sf(
    data   = sites_sf,
    aes(size = count),
    colour = "#1A5276",
    alpha  = 0.75,
    shape  = 16
  ) +
  text_prov(colour = "gray40", size = 2.8) +
  scale_size_area(
    name     = "Weeks\nobserved",
    max_size = 8,
    breaks   = c(50, 100, 150, 200)
  ) +
  crs_coord() +
  labs(
    title   = "Canadian wastewater monitoring network for COVID-19",
    caption = "Point size = number of weekly COVID-19 observations per site."
  ) +
  theme_map() +
  theme(
    legend.position  = c(0.93, 0.18),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.7), colour = NA),
    legend.title     = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(colour = "gray50", size = 8,
                                    margin = margin(t = 4))
  )
Canadian wastewater monitoring sites. Point size reflects the number of observations of COVID-19 viral loads; province abbreviations from canadianmaps.

Trend overview — all sites

The plot below shows weekly COVID signal (covN2) at every monitoring site as a thin semi-transparent line, with the national average overlaid in bold. Vertical dashed lines mark approximate Omicron wave peaks for reference.


national <- ww %>%
  filter(measureid == "covN2") %>%
  group_by(weekstart) %>%
  summarize(covN2 = mean(w_avg)) %>%
  mutate(province = "National")

ggplot() +
  # Individual sites
  geom_line(
    data  = cov,
    aes(x = weekstart, y = covN2, group = site),
    colour = "gray60", alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  # National aggregate
  geom_line(
    data      = national,
    aes(x = weekstart, y = covN2),
    colour    = "#1A5276",
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  # Approximate Omicron wave peaks (BA.1, BA.2, BA.4/5)
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = as.Date(c("2022-01-09", "2022-04-03", "2022-07-17")),
    linetype   = "dashed",
    colour     = "firebrick",
    alpha      = 0.55
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(
    x       = NULL,
    y       = "Weekly average signal (copies / mL)",
    title   = "SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal — all Canadian sites",
    caption = "Gray: individual sites.  Bold blue: national aggregate.
Dashed red: approximate Omicron BA.1 / BA.2 / BA.4-5 wave peaks (source: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/irv.70197)."
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    plot.caption    = element_text(colour = "gray40", size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
Weekly SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal at all Canadian monitoring sites (COVID). Each gray line is one site; the national aggregate (bold blue) reflects the country-wide trajectory.

Covid was detected in 99.8% of weeks.

Trends by province

With 76 sites on a single panel the regional variation is hard to see. The faceted plot below shows one line per site within each province, making it easier to assess synchrony and heterogeneity of outbreaks.


# Provinces with at least some data — ordered W → E then territories
province_order <- c(
  "British Columbia", "Alberta", "Saskatchewan", "Manitoba",
  "Ontario", "Quebec",
  "New Brunswick", "Nova Scotia", "Prince Edward Island",
  "Newfoundland and Labrador",
  "Yukon"
)

cov_prov <- cov %>%
  filter(!is.na(province)) %>%
  mutate(province = factor(province, levels = province_order))

ggplot(cov_prov, aes(x = weekstart, y = covN2,
                     colour = site, group = site)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.55, alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ province, ncol = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Weekly average signal (copies / mL)",
    title = "SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal by province",
    subtitle = "Each line = one monitoring site  |  y-axis free within panel"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#D6EAF8"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal by province. Each line is one monitoring site; colour distinguishes sites within a province.

The wave structure is broadly synchronous across provinces — consistent with a national-level change in pathogen prevalence — but the magnitude and timing of individual site peaks vary, reflecting local population sizes, sampling frequency, and catchment characteristics. This heterogeneity motivates a multi-stream approach: rather than pooling sites into a single signal, the multichangepoints framework tests each site sequentially and combines the evidence in a principled way, allowing the detector to remain powerful even when only a subset of sites shows an elevated signal.

Missing Data

Some sites are missing weeks, wherein COVID-19 measurements were not taken.

missing_by_site <- cov %>%
  group_by(site) %>%
  summarize(
    first_week = min(weekstart),
    last_week  = max(weekstart),
    observed   = list(sort(unique(weekstart))),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    expected           = list(seq(first_week, last_week, by = "7 days")),
    n_expected_observed = length(expected),
    n_missing          = sum(!expected %in% observed),
    pct_missing        = round(100 * n_missing / n_expected_observed, 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(site, n_expected_observed, n_missing, pct_missing) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_expected_observed), pct_missing)

# these are the most 'complete' sites, with the longest observations and the fewest missing
head(missing_by_site, 10)
#> # A tibble: 10 × 4
#>    site                     n_expected_observed n_missing pct_missing
#>    <chr>                                  <int>     <int>       <dbl>
#>  1 Montreal North                           281         4         1.4
#>  2 Vancouver Annacis Island                 280         2         0.7
#>  3 Toronto Humber                           280         6         2.1
#>  4 Halifax Halifax                          275         5         1.8
#>  5 Toronto Ashbridges Bay                   271         5         1.8
#>  6 Vancouver Iona Island                    266         1         0.4
#>  7 Montreal South                           266         3         1.1
#>  8 Edmonton Goldbar                         266         6         2.3
#>  9 Saskatoon                                260         2         0.8
#> 10 Winnipeg North End                       257         1         0.4

To demonstrate change-point detection on this data, we will perform such initial data filtering and transformation. For filtering we: - Select the highest-quality sites based on observable data: those that were enrolled early, contained few missing weeks, and had RSV measured. - Remove early measurements: monitoring began in 2020, but the signals are very volatile and not all sites we selected were online by then. For instance, laboratory protocols changed in February 2021 to sample the solid rather than liquid portion of a sample. RSV monitoring did not come online until January 2023. For now, we will start the streams from that date. - Remove low outliers: a very few observations (~0.1%) are zero or nearly zero. They are problematic on the log-scale and represent substantial negative deviations from the background rates observed around them. We set these to NA and linearly interpolate them when we fill in other ‘natural’ missing values.

selected_sites <- c(
  "Montreal North", 
  "Montreal South",
  "Vancouver Lions Gate",
  "Vancouver Annacis Island",
  "Winnipeg West End",
  "Winnipeg North End",
  "Toronto Humber",
  "Toronto Ashbridges Bay",
  "Toronto North Toronto",
  "Halifax Halifax",
  "St John's",
  "Edmonton Goldbar",
  "Regina"
  )

cov <- cov %>% 
  filter(site %in% selected_sites) %>% # restrict to handful of sites
  filter(weekstart > as.Date('2023-01-01')) %>% # remove early measurements
  mutate(covN2 = ifelse(covN2 > .01, covN2, NA)) # set low outliers (0.1% of values) to missing
ggplot(cov, aes(x = weekstart, y = covN2,
                     colour = site, group = site)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.55, alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ site, ncol = 3, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Weekly average signal (copies / mL)",
    title = "SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal by site",
    subtitle = "Each line = one monitoring site  |  y-axis free within panel"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#D6EAF8"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

We can also collapse across years and plot the signals by Epi-week, again on a Log-10 scale. This perspective reveals the seasonality in the timeseries: the concentrations are lower in the summer and higher in the winter.

ggplot(cov, aes(x = lubridate::epiweek(weekstart), y = covN2,
                     colour = site, group = site)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ site, ncol = 3, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Weekly average signal (copies / mL)",
    title = "SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal by site",
    subtitle = "Each line = one monitoring site  |  y-axis free within panel"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#D6EAF8"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

# Full weekly spine: every 7 days from global min to global max
all_weeks <- seq(min(cov$weekstart), max(cov$weekstart), by = "7 days")

joint_matrix <- cov %>%
  mutate(change = covN2/lag(covN2)) %>% # week-over-week change
  select(site, weekstart, covN2) %>%
  complete(site, weekstart = all_weeks) %>%   # pad all site × week combinations with NA
  arrange(site, weekstart) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = weekstart, values_from = covN2) %>%
  column_to_rownames("site") %>%
  as.matrix()

vis_miss(as.data.frame(joint_matrix))

# For each site (row), linearly interpolate only between the first and last
# observed (non-NA) value. NAs outside that window remain NA (site offline).
impute_row <- function(row) {
  obs <- which(!is.na(row))
  if (length(obs) < 2L) return(row)   # 0 or 1 observation: nothing to interpolate
  first <- obs[1L]; last <- obs[length(obs)]
  row[first:last] <- approx(obs, row[obs], xout = first:last)$y
  row
}

joint_matrix_complete <- t(apply(joint_matrix, 1, impute_row))

cat("NAs before imputation:", sum(is.na(joint_matrix)), "\n")
#> NAs before imputation: 109
cat("NAs after imputation: ", sum(is.na(joint_matrix_complete)), "\n")
#> NAs after imputation:  85
cat("(Remaining NAs are leading/trailing offline periods.)\n")
#> (Remaining NAs are leading/trailing offline periods.)

vis_miss(as.data.frame(joint_matrix_complete))

Change-point detection for WBE

Change-point detection with WBE data is a major challenge because it is very difficult to identify the state of control that is supposed to be represented by the pre-change model. In this case, the state of control is a site-specific simple or composite model from which the observed sequence is generated when there is no epidemic. Since we have multiple sites, we might try to make some kind of ‘synthetic state of control’ by synthesizing data across sites that were epidemic-free at various times. Unfortunately, this is subject to the many uncertainties inherent in wastewater surveillance across multiple sites Joung et al (2023), including:

  • Target quantity: The true target quantity of interest is the average viral load in a particular population week over week. This quantity varies naturally over time when the virus is in an endemic state. While this variation is not genuinely random, it can be modeled as such. For instance, we might assume it maintains some low-level average with as-if-Gaussian fluctuations from week to week, in which case the sequence is stationary in the endemic phase.
  • Sampling: The methods of sample collection, shipping, and storage (compositing vs grab sampling, location, timing, shipping temperature, distance to lab, missing data), which may vary across sites and time. The sites that were selected for our purposes primarily used composite sampling.
  • Analysis: Laboratory analysis variability (de-gritting subsampling, homogenization, instrument error, human error).
  • Infrastructure: The location and engineering of sewage systems and wastewater treatment plants (catchment area size and population, combined or separate (‘sanitary’) collection of runoff and wastewater).
  • Weather: Weather conditions (rainwater or snowmelt contributing to influent, temperature, pH), which vary across sites and seasons, with some degree of stochasticity.

In the literature, these uncertainties are dealt with under the rubric of normalization, which is an active darea of research. In some sense, defining a proper state of control from which deviations are meaningful is the whole challenge of WBE. It may be easier to define the state of control for certain sites, like those with sanitary sewage systems that are less influenced by major weather events.

For any given site, we could imagine an ideal way to establish its state of control via potential outcomes. In one potential outcome, the site experiences a COVID epidemic and in another epidemic the site experiences no COVID epidemic (or some kind of endemic state, both of these are very rough definitions). This is, of course, impossible in reality; we cannot even imagine a reasonable experiment because there is no way to manipulate the hypothetical intervention ‘COVID epidemic’. This could be considered a problem in observational causal inference. One way to establish the state of control is through some kind of matching, wherein we find pairs site that are similar along various axes likely to effect background rates (collection, engineering, weather, etc); but in one site there is no epidemic and in another site there is an epidemic. We could also do this by site-periods, with sites serving as their own control over periods where they are epidemic free. Another way is to fit models for the state of control. This is attempted below. In either case, establishing a meaningful state of control (a simple or composite pre-change model) should be done separately from running a change-point algorithm, which can detect deviations but cannot define the state of control without a user.

Joint wastewater site monitoring

To pass the wastewater data through the CPD algorithm, we need the site data as a matrix with NA where there are missing values. The multichangepoints software is designed to accommodate leading NAs for sites that come online late.

State of control

The appropriate state of control is essentially a causal inference problem, we imagine a potential sequence with no epidemic and a potential sequence with epidemic for each site. We observe an interleaving of these sequences, with measurement error, and wish to identify when we switch between these states with little delay and with continuous monitoring.

The details of the state of control differ site-by-site, because sources of uncertainty vary across sites. We log-transform the data and estimate the state of control separately at each site from held-out sections where we know COVID-19 levels were low in the population. We then assume that these are representative, in the sense that the distributions modeling them are stationary.

Seasonality

We remove seasonality by defining a cubic B-spline for each site with two interior knots, capturing the broad two-stage pattern visible in the data: concentrations are elevated in winter and depressed in summer.

The two knots are placed at the start of Spring (March 1, EpiWeek 9) and the start of Fall (September 1, EpiWeek 35), following PHAC / Environment Canada meteorological convention. With degree-3 B-splines and boundary knots at EpiWeeks 1 and 52, this yields 5 basis functions — enough flexibility to trace the annual high-winter / low-summer cycle without over-fitting.

We fit a hierarchical model: a population-level cubic B-spline captures the shared seasonal shape, and each site gets its own random deviation on each spline coefficient (partially pooled across sites via independent random effects, one variance per basis function). The response is \(\log_{10}(\text{covN2})\).

library(splines)
library(lme4)

# Two interior knots at the two season transitions driving the annual cycle
# (PHAC / Environment Canada meteorological convention):
#   Spring = Mar 1 ~ EpiWk 9  |  Fall = Sep 1 ~ EpiWk 35
season_knots <- c(9, 35)

# ---- Stack imputed matrix to long format ------------------------------------
ww_model <- joint_matrix_complete %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("site") %>%
  pivot_longer(-site, names_to = "weekstart_chr", values_to = "covN2") %>%
  mutate(
    weekstart   = as.Date(weekstart_chr),
    log10_covN2 = log10(covN2),
    EpiWeek     = lubridate::epiweek(weekstart),
    EpiYear     = lubridate::epiyear(weekstart),
    site        = factor(site)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(log10_covN2)) %>%
  select(-weekstart_chr)

# ---- B-spline basis with seasonal interior knots ----------------------------
# degree = 3 (cubic); boundary knots = EpiWeeks 1 and 52
B <- bs(ww_model$EpiWeek,
        knots          = season_knots,
        degree         = 3,
        Boundary.knots = c(1, 52))
spl_cols <- paste0("spl", seq_len(ncol(B)))
colnames(B) <- spl_cols
ww_model <- bind_cols(ww_model, as_tibble(B))

# ---- Hierarchical lmer: fixed overall seasonal shape + per-site random ------
# (0 + splK || site) = independent (diagonal) random effects on each basis
# coefficient — partial pooling without assuming correlation across basis fns.
fixed_rhs <- paste(spl_cols, collapse = " + ")
rand_rhs  <- paste(sprintf("(0 + %s | site)", spl_cols), collapse = " + ")
model_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("log10_covN2 ~", fixed_rhs, "+", rand_rhs)
)

fit_seasonal <- lmer(
  model_formula,
  data    = ww_model,
  REML    = TRUE,
  control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa",
                        optCtrl   = list(maxfun = 2e5))
)

ww_model <- ww_model %>%
  mutate(
    fitted_log10 = fitted(fit_seasonal),
    resid_log10  = resid(fit_seasonal)
  )
# shared theme
theme_panel <- theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#D6EAF8"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

# Vertical guides at the two knot dates (spring and fall transitions)
season_dates <- as.Date(c("2023-03-01", "2023-09-01",
                           "2024-03-01", "2024-09-01",
                           "2025-03-01", "2025-09-01"))

# Plot 1: raw data + fitted spline
ggplot(ww_model, aes(x = weekstart, colour = site, group = site)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = log10_covN2), size = 0.8, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(aes(y = fitted_log10), linewidth = 0.9, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = season_dates, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "gray60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ site, ncol = 3, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = expression(log[10]~"signal (copies / mL)"),
    title    = "Hierarchical seasonal spline fit",
    subtitle = "Points = observed  |  Line = site-specific spline  |  Dotted = season start"
  ) +
  theme_panel
Log10 SARS-CoV-2 wastewater signal per site (points) with fitted hierarchical seasonal spline (line).
ggplot(ww_model, aes(x = weekstart, y = resid_log10,
                     colour = site, group = site)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5, colour = "gray40") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.55, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = season_dates, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "gray60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ site, ncol = 3, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = expression(log[10]~"residual"),
    title    = "Residuals from hierarchical seasonal spline",
    subtitle = "Deviations above 0 indicate signal above the seasonal baseline"
  ) +
  theme_panel
Residuals from the hierarchical seasonal spline model. Persistent positive deviations suggest epidemic signal above the seasonal baseline.

Autocorrelation in residuals

If the seasonal spline has absorbed all temporal structure, residuals should be approximately white noise within each site. We test this with (i) per-site ACF plots and (ii) the Ljung-Box portmanteau test at lag 4 (one month).

# ---- ACF plot: one panel per site ------------------------------------------
max_lag <- 20
sites_ordered <- levels(ww_model$site)

acf_df <- ww_model %>%
  group_by(site) %>%
  arrange(weekstart, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  summarise(
    acf_vals = list(acf(resid_log10, lag.max = max_lag, plot = FALSE)$acf[-1]),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(lag = list(seq_len(max_lag))) %>%
  unnest(c(acf_vals, lag))

ci <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(nrow(ww_model) / length(sites_ordered))  # approx per-site n

ggplot(acf_df, aes(x = lag, y = acf_vals, colour = site)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "gray40") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-ci, ci), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = lag, yend = 0), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ site, ncol = 3) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max_lag, by = 4)) +
  labs(
    x        = "Lag (weeks)",
    y        = "ACF",
    title    = "Residual autocorrelation by site",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines = 95% white-noise bounds"
  ) +
  theme_panel
Sample ACF of spline residuals by site. Dashed blue lines are the 95% white-noise bounds. Bars extending beyond the bounds indicate significant autocorrelation at that lag.
# Ljung-Box test at lag 4 per site (H0: no autocorrelation up to lag 4)
lb_results <- ww_model %>%
  group_by(site) %>%
  arrange(weekstart, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    lb_stat  = Box.test(resid_log10, lag = 4, type = "Ljung-Box")$statistic,
    lb_pval  = Box.test(resid_log10, lag = 4, type = "Ljung-Box")$p.value,
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      lb_pval < 0.001 ~ "***",
      lb_pval < 0.01  ~ "**",
      lb_pval < 0.05  ~ "*",
      TRUE            ~ ""
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(lb_pval)

knitr::kable(lb_results,
             digits  = c(0, 0, 2, 3, 0),
             col.names = c("Site", "n", "LB statistic", "p-value", ""),
             caption = "Ljung-Box test for residual autocorrelation (lag = 4). Significant p-values indicate remaining temporal structure not captured by the seasonal spline.")
Ljung-Box test for residual autocorrelation (lag = 4). Significant p-values indicate remaining temporal structure not captured by the seasonal spline.
Site n LB statistic p-value
Edmonton Goldbar 166 140.35 0 ***
Halifax Halifax 163 99.69 0 ***
Montreal North 166 139.90 0 ***
Regina 166 175.78 0 ***
Toronto Ashbridges Bay 166 114.70 0 ***
Toronto Humber 166 121.58 0 ***
Toronto North Toronto 140 113.62 0 ***
Vancouver Annacis Island 166 203.61 0 ***
Vancouver Lions Gate 137 131.04 0 ***
Winnipeg North End 166 124.24 0 ***
Winnipeg West End 139 241.50 0 ***
Montreal South 166 80.74 0 ***

Next steps: - Need to decide what the appropriate signal to monitor is, and what it’s ‘state of control’ should be. Probably we can pre-transform everything before monitoring, so that we are basically just tracking independent residuals. Then we can state the assumptions we need / run tests (like ACF) to ensure those properly follow the pre-change model (including independence). - Multiple testing error control (FWER, FDR)
